children.name: Cettia
children.description: Cetti's warbler and its Asian relatives — skulking, rounded passerines of dense undergrowth. Cetti's Warbler is renowned for its explosive, loud song that seems impossible for such a small, secretive bird. The genus is named after Italian zoologist Francesco Cetti.

children.name: Cettia cetti
children.description: A stocky, chestnut-brown warbler with a rounded tail and a pale supercilium. Despite its skulking nature, its song is one of the loudest and most startling of any European bird — a sudden burst of ringing notes that erupts from dense waterside vegetation. Expanding northwards in Europe; increasingly recorded in southern Sweden. Resident year-round.

children.name: Cettia brunnea
children.description: A small bush warbler found from the Himalayas through southern China and Southeast Asia. Warm brown above, pale below with brown flanks. Skulks in thick undergrowth, often near water. Its song is a series of clear, descending whistles.

children.name: Cettia castaneocoronata
children.description: A tiny, brightly coloured warbler of Himalayan and Southeast Asian montane forests with a vivid chestnut crown and nape, olive-green upperparts, and bright yellow underparts. It skulks in dense undergrowth, constantly flicking its wings, and its high-pitched song is often the only clue to its presence. It is more terrestrial than most cettias, hopping mouse-like through the leaf litter.

children.name: Cisticola
children.description: Cisticolas — a large genus of small, streaky warblers found across Africa, Asia, and southern Europe. They are named for their characteristic 'cisticola' buzzing or trilling songs, often given in aerial displays above grassland. Over 50 species, many of which are notoriously difficult to identify in the field.

children.name: Cisticola juncidis
children.description: A tiny, streaky brown bird of wet grasslands and rice paddies. Its name comes from its song — a monotonous, repeated 'zit-zit-zit' given in a distinctive bouncing display flight. One of the most widespread Old World passerines. Occurs around the Mediterranean and is expanding northwards.

children.name: Cisticola erlangeri
children.description: Endemic to the Ethiopian highlands. Similar to the Zitting Cisticola but darker and more richly coloured. Inhabits montane grassland and moorland above 2,000 metres.

children.name: Cisticola albiscutata
children.description: Found in eastern and southern Africa. Distinctive rufous wing panels and a heavily streaked back. Inhabits moist grassland and marshes. Its display flight is accompanied by a dry, insect-like buzzing.

children.name: Cisticola exilis
children.description: A tiny cisticola in which the breeding male develops a striking golden-yellow head and breast contrasting with a streaked brown back. Found from India through Southeast Asia to the Philippines, New Guinea, and eastern Australia. Its song is a buzzing trill given during a circling display flight above grassland and marshland.

children.name: Cisticola galactotes
children.description: A medium-sized cisticola of moist African grasslands and marshes, with a heavily streaked back and a warm rufous crown. Its song, a long, winding series of notes that rises and falls in pitch, gives it its common name. It is often seen perched on grass stems or doing a low, fluttering display flight.

children.name: Cisticola carruthersi
children.description: A swamp-dwelling cisticola found in papyrus marshes and wetlands of East Africa, from Uganda to Tanzania. It is dark and heavily streaked with a rufous crown and nape. Its song is a rapid, metallic trill. Named after the ornithologist who documented its distinctiveness from the Winding Cisticola.

children.name: Cisticola robustus
children.description: A large, robust cisticola of montane grasslands and moorlands across eastern and central Africa. Dark brown above with heavy streaking, a rufous crown, and a stout bill that distinguishes it from other highland cisticolas. Its song is a loud, far-carrying series of whistles and buzzes.

children.name: Cisticola cantans
children.description: A common cisticola of moist savanna and montane grasslands across sub-Saharan Africa. Warm brown above with dark streaking, pale below with a buff wash. Its song — a distinctive, repetitive three-note whistle — is one of the most characteristic sounds of African highland grasslands. It perches conspicuously on tall grass stems and low bushes.

children.name: Cisticola subruficapilla
children.description: Endemic to southern Africa, this small cisticola has a grey back, rufous crown, and pale underparts. It is common in fynbos, Karoo scrub, and arid grassland. Its song is a short, harsh buzzing, and it is often seen performing a low, fluttering display flight over open terrain.

children.name: Cisticola hunteri
children.description: A medium-sized cisticola of montane grasslands in East Africa, from Kenya to Tanzania. It has a heavily streaked back, a rufous crown, and a distinctive chattering song. Named after the ornithologist Hunter. Inhabits high-altitude grasslands above 1,800 metres.

children.name: Cisticola nigriloris
children.description: A distinctive cisticola with a black loral patch and forecrown, a rufous crown, and dark-streaked brown upperparts. Found in the montane grasslands of Tanzania and Malawi. Its song is a series of metallic, buzzing notes given during a display flight.

children.name: Cisticola melanurus
children.description: A small cisticola with a distinctive black tail and finely streaked upperparts. Endemic to the grasslands of Angola and the southern Democratic Republic of Congo. It inhabits moist Brachystegia woodland and dambo grasslands, where it perches conspicuously on grass stems.

children.name: Cisticola anderseni
children.description: A recently described cisticola from the floodplains of Tanzania, distinguished from the Winding Cisticola by its white-tipped tail feathers and different vocalisations. It is restricted to seasonally flooded grasslands and is named after the Danish ornithologist Knud Andersen.

children.name: Cisticola aberrans
children.description: A robust, long-tailed cisticola of rocky hillsides and boulder-strewn grasslands across sub-Saharan Africa. It has unstreaked grey-brown upperparts and a pale throat. Its name comes from its preference for rocky habitats, where it hops nimbly among boulders and gives its distinctive, descending trilling song.

children.name: Cisticola njombe
children.description: A montane cisticola endemic to the highlands of southern Tanzania, around the Njombe region. It is named for its distinctive churring song, a rapid, insect-like trill. It inhabits moist montane grasslands above 2,000 metres and is locally common but restricted in range.

children.name: Cisticola lais
children.description: A medium-sized cisticola of montane and moist grasslands across eastern and southern Africa. It has a streaked brown back, a rufous crown, and a distinctive wailing song that descends in pitch. It is common in highland grasslands and often perches on tall grass stems, giving its plaintive, far-carrying call.

children.name: Cisticola anonymus
children.description: A small, chattering cisticola of moist lowland grasslands and marshes in West and Central Africa. It is warm brown above with dark streaking and has an incessant, chattering song that gives it its name. It is common in wet grasslands and rice paddies from Sierra Leone to the Democratic Republic of Congo.

children.name: Prinia
children.description: Prinias — long-tailed, active warblers of Africa and Asia, often called 'long-tailed warblers'. They hold their slender tails cocked at an angle and flick them characteristically. About 25 species, many of which have adapted well to human-modified habitats like farmland and gardens.

children.name: Prinia gracilis
children.description: A delicate, pale prinia with a very long, graduated tail. Buff-brown above, whitish below. Found across the Middle East, South Asia, and northeastern Africa. Inhabits dry scrub, tamarisk thickets, and reedbeds. Its tail is constantly in motion, wagging and fanning.

children.name: Prinia socialis
children.description: A common resident of the Indian subcontinent. Slaty-grey above in breeding plumage, warmer brown in winter. Chestnut wing panel. Inhabits scrub, grassland, and gardens. One of the few prinias with distinct breeding and non-breeding plumages.

children.name: Prinia subflava
children.description: The most widespread African prinia. Warm brown upperparts, buffy-white underparts, and a long cocked tail. Found across sub-Saharan Africa in grassland, scrub, and cultivation. Its song is a rapid, repetitive trill.

children.name: Prinia cinereocapilla
children.description: A striking prinia with a grey crown, warm brown upperparts, and whitish underparts. It is restricted to the Himalayan foothills from northern India to Nepal and Bhutan. Inhabits scrubby grasslands and forest edges, where its sharp, repeated calls give away its presence as it flits through tangled vegetation.

children.name: Prinia superciliaris
children.description: A large, long-tailed prinia of montane forests from the eastern Himalayas to Southeast Asia. Dark brown above with a prominent white supercilium, greyish underparts, and a very long graduated tail. It inhabits dense undergrowth in hill and montane forests, where its loud, chattering song carries far.

children.name: Prinia rufescens
children.description: A small, warm brown prinia with a rufous tint to the upperparts and a long, graduated tail. Ranges from the eastern Himalayas through Myanmar to Indochina and the Malay Peninsula. Inhabits scrub, grass, and forest clearings in lowland and hill areas. Its song is a series of high-pitched, insect-like notes.

children.name: Prinia inornata
children.description: A drab, plain-looking prinia with a long tail and unstreaked brown upperparts. Found across South and Southeast Asia from Pakistan and India to China, Myanmar, and Indonesia. Inhabits wet grasslands, reedbeds, and rice paddies. Its song is a monotonous, repeated trill that is a common sound in Asian wetlands.

children.name: Prinia sylvatica
children.description: A large, robust prinia of scrub and secondary forest in the Indian subcontinent. Rich brown above with a long, broad tail that is often held cocked. Its song is a loud, explosive series of notes. It is more secretive than other prinias, typically staying hidden in dense thickets.

children.name: Prinia flavicans
children.description: A striking prinia of southern Africa with a bold black breast band contrasting with white underparts and a pale grey-brown back. Found in arid savanna, scrub, and woodland edges. Its song is a rapid, high-pitched trill, and it is often seen perching conspicuously on top of bushes with its tail cocked vertically.

children.name: Prinia crinigera
children.description: A large prinia with heavily streaked upperparts, a white supercilium, and a very long, broad tail. Found from the Himalayas through Myanmar to southern China and Taiwan. Inhabits scrub, grassland, and forest edges at middle to high elevations. Its distinctive chattering song and habit of perching on exposed grass stems make it relatively easy to observe.

children.name: Prinia flaviventris
children.description: A small prinia with olive-green upperparts, a bright yellow belly, and a long graduated tail. Ranges from the Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Sumatra and Borneo. Inhabits wet grasslands, reeds, and scrub near water. Its song is a monotonous, buzzy trill, and it actively flits through vegetation flicking its tail.

children.name: Prinia familiaris
children.description: A distinctive prinia endemic to Indonesia, found on Sumatra, Java, and Bali. It has grey upperparts, a long tail with white tips, and striking white wing bars that are visible in flight. It inhabits mangroves, coastal scrub, and gardens. Its song is a series of musical trills and whistles.

children.name: Prinia fluviatilis
children.description: A medium-sized prinia found along rivers and lakeshores in the Sahel zone of Africa, from Senegal to Ethiopia. It has pale grey-brown upperparts, whitish underparts, and a long tail. It inhabits reedbeds, papyrus swamps, and riverside vegetation, where its song — a series of rapid, high-pitched trills — is a characteristic sound of African wetlands.

children.name: Prinia lepida
children.description: A small, very pale prinia with finely streaked upperparts and a long, graduated tail. Found in arid and semi-arid regions from the Middle East to Pakistan and northwestern India. Inhabits dry scrub, tamarisk thickets, and desert wadis. Its delicate build and pale plumage help it blend into its arid surroundings.

children.name: Schistolais
children.description: A small genus of prinia-like warblers found in Africa, formerly included in Prinia but separated on molecular grounds. They have relatively short tails for prinias and are restricted to montane and submontane regions of West and East Africa.

children.name: Schistolais leucopogon
children.description: A greyish-brown warbler with a distinctive white chin and throat, a long tail, and pale underparts. Found in the montane forests of West and Central Africa, from Nigeria to Uganda and Angola. Inhabits forest edges, clearings, and secondary growth at elevations above 1,000 metres. Its song is a series of rapid, chattering notes.

children.name: Oreolais
children.description: A small genus of apalis-like warblers restricted to the mountains of Central Africa. They are small, active birds of montane forest undergrowth and bamboo thickets, with distinctive black-and-white or grey-and-white plumage patterns.

children.name: Oreolais pulcher
children.description: A small, active warbler with a striking black collar across the white throat, grey upperparts, and white underparts. Found in the highlands of Cameroon and Nigeria. Inhabits montane forest, bamboo thickets, and forest edges above 1,500 metres. Its song is a thin, high-pitched trill.

children.name: Oreolais ruwenzorii
children.description: A small warbler endemic to the Rwenzori Mountains of Uganda and the Democratic Republic of Congo. It has grey upperparts, a white throat, and black breast band similar to the Black-collared Apalis. Inhabits montane bamboo forest and giant heath zones between 2,000 and 3,500 metres.

children.name: Apalis
children.description: A large genus of small, active warblers found predominantly in sub-Saharan Africa, with a few species extending into the Arabian Peninsula. They are slender, long-tailed birds of forest and woodland, often foraging in mixed-species flocks. Many species show striking throat colouration in shades of yellow, orange, chestnut, or black.

children.name: Apalis thoracica
children.description: A highly variable, widely distributed apalis of eastern and southern Africa, with a distinctive black or grey breast band across the yellow throat. Found in montane and coastal forests, woodland, and well-wooded gardens from Kenya to South Africa. Its song is a rapid, repetitive trill.

children.name: Apalis flavigularis
children.description: A small, brightly coloured apalis with a bright yellow throat and breast, grey upperparts, and a long tail. Endemic to the montane forests of southern Malawi and adjacent Mozambique. Classified as Endangered due to deforestation and fragmentation of its highland forest habitat.

children.name: Apalis rufogularis
children.description: A medium-sized apalis with a warm buff throat and breast, olive-grey upperparts, and a long graduated tail. Widespread across the forests of Central and West Africa from Nigeria to Uganda and Angola. Inhabits lowland and montane forests, often in mixed-species flocks in the mid-storey.

children.name: Apalis porphyrolaema
children.description: A striking apalis with a rich chestnut throat and breast, grey upperparts, and white belly. Found in the montane forests of East Africa, from Kenya to Tanzania and the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo. Inhabits the canopy and edges of montane forests above 1,500 metres.

children.name: Apalis fuscigularis
children.description: A small, dark-throated apalis endemic to the Ethiopian highlands. It has dark grey upperparts, a blackish throat and upper breast, and white belly. Restricted to the Tacazze River region and nearby montane forests, where it is classified as Endangered due to habitat loss.

children.name: Apalis cinerea
children.description: A plain, grey-and-white apalis with pale grey upperparts, white underparts, and a relatively short tail. Found across West and Central Africa from Sierra Leone to Uganda and Angola. Inhabits lowland and montane forests, often foraging in the canopy in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Apalis chariessa
children.description: A rare and striking apalis with a black breast band, yellow throat and belly, grey upperparts, and distinctive white patches on the wings. Found in Kenya and Tanzania, with a disjunct population in West Africa. Inhabits lowland and foothill forests, where it forages in the canopy. Classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Camaroptera
children.description: The camaropteras, or green-backed warblers — a small genus of stocky, short-tailed warblers found across sub-Saharan Africa. They are stout, active birds of undergrowth and forest edges, often flicking their wings and cocking their short tails. Their songs are typically loud, repeated phrases or trills.

children.name: Camaroptera brachyura
children.description: A small, stocky warbler with bright olive-green upperparts, pale grey underparts, and a very short tail that it often holds cocked. Widespread across sub-Saharan Africa in forest edges, scrub, and gardens. Its loud, repetitive song — a series of ringing notes — is one of the most familiar bird sounds in African woodland.

children.name: Camaroptera superciliaris
children.description: A small camaroptera with a distinctive bright yellow eyebrow stripe, olive-green upperparts, and pale yellow underparts. Found in West and Central Africa from Sierra Leone to Uganda. Inhabits lowland forest undergrowth and forest edges, where it skulks in dense vegetation, constantly flicking its wings.

children.name: Camaroptera chloronota
children.description: A uniformly olive-green camaroptera with a pale throat and belly, and a short, dark tail. Found across the forests of West and Central Africa, from Guinea to Uganda and Angola. It inhabits the understorey of lowland and montane forests, where its loud, dry scratching song is often the only indication of its presence in dense cover.

children.name: Calamonastes
children.description: A genus of wren-warblers found in eastern and southern Africa, with finely barred plumage and relatively long tails. They are skulking birds of scrub, miombo woodland, and dry thickets, more often heard than seen. Their songs are dry, insect-like trills.

children.name: Calamonastes simplex
children.description: A small, grey-brown warbler with fine dark barring on the underparts and a relatively long tail. Found in the dry savannas and miombo woodlands of East Africa, from Ethiopia to Tanzania. It is a skulking bird of dense thickets and scrub, often difficult to observe, but its distinctive churring song betrays its presence.

children.name: Calamonastes undosus
children.description: A small, finely barred warbler of miombo woodlands in south-central Africa, from Angola to Tanzania and Mozambique. It has grey-brown upperparts, barred underparts, and a long tail. It skulks in the understorey and mid-storey of miombo forest, where its dry, insect-like song can be heard throughout the day.

children.name: Calamonastes stierlingi
children.description: A small wren-warbler of miombo woodlands in southeastern Africa, from Tanzania to Mozambique. It has brown upperparts, finely barred underparts, and a long, graduated tail. Named after the German naturalist Carl Stierling. It inhabits the understorey of Brachystegia woodland and is more often heard than seen.

children.name: Eremomela
children.description: A genus of small, active warblers of Africa, often called eremomelas or yellow-bellied warblers. They are found in savanna, woodland, and arid scrub, where they forage actively in small flocks, gleaning insects from foliage. Several species have bright yellow underparts contrasting with grey or green upperparts.

children.name: Eremomela icteropygialis
children.description: A small, active warbler with grey-brown upperparts, a bright yellow belly and vent, and a pale throat. Widespread across sub-Saharan Africa in dry savanna, scrub, and acacia woodland. It is often seen in small, restless flocks foraging in the foliage of trees and bushes, constantly calling with thin, high-pitched notes.

children.name: Eremomela scotops
children.description: A small, green-capped warbler with olive-green upperparts, a yellow throat and belly, and a distinctive greyish face. Found across sub-Saharan Africa in woodland and savanna, from Senegal to Ethiopia and south to South Africa. It is an active, acrobatic forager, often hanging upside-down while gleaning insects from leaves.

children.name: Eremomela badiceps
children.description: A small warbler with a distinctive rufous-chestnut crown, olive-green upperparts, and pale yellow underparts. Found in the forests of West and Central Africa, from Sierra Leone to Uganda and Angola. Inhabits the canopy and edges of lowland and montane forests, often in mixed-species foraging flocks.

children.name: Hemitesia
children.description: A small genus of bush warblers from Africa, with a disjunct distribution in the highlands of East Africa and lowland forests of West Africa. They are skulking, short-tailed warblers that inhabit dense undergrowth and forest floor vegetation. Their songs are high-pitched and insect-like, and they are more often heard than seen.

children.name: Hemitesia pallidipes
children.description: A shy, subtly plumaged bush warbler found in the undergrowth of East African montane forests. It has olive-brown upperparts, pale grey underparts, and notably pale pinkish legs that give it its name. Its song is a thin, high-pitched trill that is difficult to locate, and it prefers dense thickets where it forages close to the ground.

children.name: Horornis
children.description: An Asian genus of bush warblers formerly included in Cettia. They are small, cryptically plumaged birds of dense undergrowth in forests and scrublands, with a distribution from the Himalayas through East and Southeast Asia. They are renowned for their loud, carrying songs that seem disproportionate to their size.

children.name: Horornis flavolivaceus
children.description: A plain olive-green bush warbler with a pale yellowish throat and belly, found from the Himalayas through Myanmar and southern China to northern Vietnam. Despite its common name, it is typical of the bush warblers in its skulking behaviour and loud, distinctive song — a ringing series of whistles. It inhabits dense scrub, bamboo thickets, and forest edges at middle elevations.
